low: Develop and adopt a formal code of conduct for all employees and stakeholders, aligned with OECD integrity guidelines
Establish an ethics committee or appoint an integrity officer with a clear mandate and direct reporting line to senior leadership
Implement mandatory ethics and integrity training for all senior leaders and board members
medium: Strengthen board-level oversight of integrity programmes by establishing a dedicated governance committee with regular reporting cycles
Publish an annual ethics and integrity report detailing key metrics, incidents, and improvement actions
Conduct regular integrity culture surveys to assess employee perceptions and identify areas for improvement
high: Benchmark your governance framework against ISO 37001 and ISO 37002 standards to identify further refinement opportunities
Pursue external certification of your integrity management system and share best practices through peer learning networks

low: Draft and implement a comprehensive anti-bribery and anti-corruption policy covering all forms of corruption, including facilitation payments
Establish a conflict-of-interest declaration process for all employees, with mandatory annual updates
Introduce basic corruption risk assessment procedures for high-risk business areas and geographic regions
medium: Implement risk-based due diligence procedures for all third parties, suppliers, and business partners with documented outcomes
Develop a centralised gifts and hospitality register with clear thresholds, approval workflows, and regular management review
Conduct sector-specific corruption risk assessments annually and integrate findings into enterprise risk management
high: Adopt continuous monitoring tools and data analytics to detect corruption red flags across procurement and financial transactions
Engage in collective action initiatives with industry peers and join integrity pacts for major public contracts

low: Begin publishing basic financial reports and key organisational data in accessible formats on your website
Establish minimum disclosure standards for procurement processes, including publishing contract award notices
Create a public register of senior officials' interests and asset declarations
medium: Adopt open data standards for procurement and financial reporting to enhance comparability and public access
Publish a comprehensive annual transparency report covering governance decisions, lobbying contacts, and policy-making processes
Implement beneficial ownership disclosure requirements for all entities involved in public contracts
high: Deploy real-time public dashboards for procurement, budgeting, and contract execution aligned with Open Contracting Data Standards
Participate in international transparency initiatives such as the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative or Open Government Partnership

low: Establish at least one secure reporting channel for employees and external parties to report suspected misconduct
Develop a formal whistleblower protection policy that prohibits all forms of retaliation, in line with the EU Whistleblower Protection Directive
Designate a trained person or team responsible for receiving, assessing, and responding to whistleblower reports
medium: Implement multiple reporting channels including an anonymous online portal and an independent external hotline
Develop standardised investigation procedures with defined timelines, qualified investigators, and feedback mechanisms for reporters
Conduct regular training for all staff on whistleblower rights, reporting procedures, and anti-retaliation protections
high: Commission an independent third party to operate and audit your whistleblower reporting and investigation system
Publish anonymised aggregate data on reports received, investigations conducted, and outcomes achieved to demonstrate accountability

low: Establish basic internal audit procedures for integrity and compliance programmes with regular reporting to management
Create a centralised incident register to track all integrity-related incidents, complaints, and their resolution status
Define clear disciplinary procedures for integrity violations that apply consistently regardless of seniority
medium: Implement a compliance monitoring programme with defined KPIs, regular assessments, and centralised tracking across all departments
Develop a formal corrective action process with root-cause analysis, assigned responsibilities, deadlines, and effectiveness verification
Commission periodic external audits or peer reviews of your integrity management system
high: Deploy an integrated compliance management system with automated monitoring, real-time dashboards, and predictive analytics for risk identification
Establish a continuous improvement framework that systematically captures lessons learned and feeds them back into policy and programme updates

low: Establish basic feedback mechanisms such as surveys or suggestion boxes to collect input from citizens and service users on integrity matters
Initiate contact with relevant civil society organisations and anti-corruption watchdog bodies to explore potential collaboration
Publish information about public consultation opportunities and how stakeholders can participate in governance processes
medium: Develop structured public consultation processes with documented responses to stakeholder input and published outcomes
Create formal partnerships with at least two civil society organisations or academic institutions focused on governance and integrity
Implement targeted outreach programmes to ensure participation of underrepresented communities in integrity-related decision-making
high: Establish multi-stakeholder governance bodies such as citizen advisory panels or integrity co-design forums with meaningful decision-making influence
Develop and publish an annual stakeholder engagement report with measurable impact indicators and independent evaluation of engagement quality
